metadata.title: AGI by 2030.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: this is Liam, in for Bear. Demis Hassabis—Nobel Prize winner, founder of Google DeepMind, and one of the people actively building toward AGI—has stated a compressed timeline: AGI by 2030. Maximum. Possibly earlier. Four years from now, the work you do today looks almost nothing like the work you'll be paid for. That's the claim. Here's what it actually means for your job.

props.command: Demis Hassabis says AGI by 2030. What does that actually mean for my job?

beats.narration_text: Context matters here. Demis Hassabis is not a pundit. Age four: playing chess. Age thirteen: chess master level. Age thirty-four: co-founded DeepMind. Age thirty-seven: DeepMind acquired by Google for six hundred million pounds — considered the best acquisition in history by cost-to-outcome. Age thirty-nine: led the team that built AlphaGo, which beat the world's best Go player at a game with more board configurations than atoms in the known universe. Age forty-eight: Nobel Prize in Chemistry for AlphaFold. This is who said AGI by 2030.

artifactLines: Age 13: chess master. Age 34: co-founded DeepMind.
Age 37: Google acquires DeepMind for £600M — best acquisition in history.
Age 39: AlphaGo beats world champion Go player. 10¹⁷⁰ possible board states.
Age 48: Nobel Prize in Chemistry for AlphaFold. Now building AGI.

beats.narration_text: At Google I/O on May 20th, Demis discussed the future of work. The significance is not celebrity proximity; it is that Demis isn't speculating about AGI from the outside. He's building it, from the inside, right now. His definition of AGI: when the machine becomes smarter than us, humans, in every domain that matters.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B02.png (photo of Demis Hassabis at Google I/O, or a clean timeline graphic showing the AGI-by-2030 claim)

beats.narration_text: What does AGI actually mean in practice? Compare marketing work across generations. In 1984, market research in Paris could mean working without spreadsheets. Before dashboards. Before Google Forms. The job meant walking into the street, stopping strangers, asking what they thought of a product, writing answers in a notebook, typing them up later, building the report. Forty years later, the same field looks completely different. AGI is that kind of discontinuity — except compressed from four decades to four years.

artifactLines: 1984, Paris: marketing = walking the street, stopping strangers, notebook + typewriter.
2022: same job, different planet — dashboards, forms, digital everything.
That transformation took 40 years. AGI compresses it to 4.
DeepMind's mission: 'Solve intelligence, then use that to solve everything else.'

beats.narration_text: The useful response to an aggressive AGI timeline is urgency rather than fear. Researchers such as Ilya Sutskever, Andrej Karpathy, and Demis Hassabis matter here because they helped shape the systems being evaluated—not because proximity to them proves the forecast. The move for everyone is the same whether Demis is right or wrong.

props.artifactTitle: The practical frame — urgency, not fear
artifactLines: The broader frame: Sutskever, Karpathy, Pressfield, Rubin, Greene — and Demis.
Demis is building AGI from inside the lab. Not speculating. Building.
If he's right: 4 years. If he's wrong: a few more years to prepare.
Either way: the move is the same.

beats.narration_text: What is the move? The idea is not presented with a tidy checklist — but the whole body of the body of work implies it clearly. Get fluent with AI now, while the gap between users and non-users is still large enough to matter. Build your workflow around AI tools so the transition to more powerful versions is incremental, not catastrophic. And stop treating AI as a productivity shortcut and start treating it as the new baseline cost of doing knowledge work at all. The people who figure this out in 2026 are not scrambling in 2030.

artifactLines: Get fluent while the fluency gap is still large enough to matter.
Build your workflow around AI — make upgrades incremental, not catastrophic.
Stop treating AI as a shortcut. It's the new floor of knowledge work.
Fluent in 2026 → not scrambling in 2030. That's the whole thesis.

beats.narration_text: Andrej Karpathy is worth a footnote here because it is significant: Karpathy, one of the original architects of GPT at OpenAI, just joined Anthropic — the company behind Claude. The people who shaped the architecture of the most transformative AI systems are now concentrating at Anthropic. Whether that changes the competitive landscape between Claude and GPT is a separate question, but it's a signal worth tracking if you're placing bets on which AI ecosystem to get fluent in.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B06.png (screenshot of news coverage or announcement of Andrej Karpathy joining Anthropic, or his LinkedIn/X post about the move)

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Ask Claude to stress-test the AGI-by-2030 claim against your specific job. This is the most useful thing you can do with this conversation today.

props.command: I work in [your field / role]. If AGI arrives by 2030, what parts of my job are most at risk of being automated, and what skills should I be building right now to stay relevant? Ask me clarifying questions first.

beats.narration_text: Four years. Same move either way. Start now.

props.title: AGI by 2030.
props.subline: Fluent now. Ready in four years.
